papers.full_text: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”. Similar content being viewed by others Introduction On May 2, 2026, a deadly outbreak of hantavirus infection on the cruise ship MV Hondius in the Atlantic Ocean was reported. At that time, according to the ship operator, 147 passengers and crew members remained onboard, while 34 individuals had previously disembarked. By 13 May 2026, the ongoing outbreak had resulted in a total of 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, corresponding to a case fatality rate of approximately 27%.The early reported cases were laboratory-confirmed as Andes virus (ANDV) infections [1]. ANDV is a member of the family Hantaviridae .Other members of the family are Hantaan virus (HTNV), Seoul virus (SEOV), Dobrava-Belgrade virus (DOBV), Puumala virus (PUUV) and Sin Nombre virus (SNV) and all are highly pathogenic to humans [2]. News and reports about this outbreak drew attention of the public as well as the scientific community and an important question emerged, Can this outbreak on the cruise lead to a pandemic? In this article, we will review the current knowledge on hantaviruses, and lessons from the past pandemics, trying to find the answer for this important question (Fig. 1). May 13 : 11 cases, including 3 deaths. - A: April 1, Argentina:Departure with no reported symptomatic passengers. - B: At sea, April 6 :Index case symptoms, 70 year old Dutch man , April 11 died from respiratory distress. - C: St.Helena, April 24 :23 passengers disembark to return to their countries.The wife of the index case (now the 2nd case) disembark to Johannesburg. - D: Johannesburg, April 27, 2nd case died, 3rd case confirmed. - E: At sea, May 2, 4th case died on board. - F: At sea, May 4, two new cases suspected. - G: Switzerland, May 4, one confirmed case. - H: Netherlands, May 6, two confirmed cases. - I: France, May 10, one confirmed case. - J: United sataes, May 10, one confirmed case. History of hantaviruses During the Korean Peninsula war (from 1950 till1953), about three thousands of the soldier of the United Nations Command developed hemorrhagic fever and features of acute kidney injury, that was then described as hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS). The definite etiology of this disease was not discovered until 1981, when Lee et al. described HTNV, and its presence in the lungs of the striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius). Nephropathia epidemica (NE), a condition resembling HFRS but less severe, was described in Fennoscandia since the early 1930s, but its causative virus, the PUUV was discovered in rodents bank voles in the year 1980 in Finland [2]. In 1993, an outbreak of a severe pulmonary condition occurred in the Four Corners region of the USA, with a rapidly progressive course resulting in adult respiratory distress syndrome.This was discovered to be a result of infection with a new hantavirus the SNV and the disease was termed hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) [3]. This was the first description of this syndrome and the first identification of hantavirus diseases in the Americas. In subsequent years, additional hantaviruses responsible for HCPS were identified throughout the Americas, with ANDV being recognized in 1995. The first documented man-to-man transmission of ANDV occurred during an outbreak in El Bolsón, Argentina, in 1996. Since that event, several sporadic outbreaks involving interpersonal transmission have also been described [4]. Currently, more than 28 hantavirus species pathogenic to humans have been identified globally, causing a broad clinical spectrum ranging from acute kidney injury and pulmonary edema to severe hemorrhagic manifestations. Although approximately 1,000 HCPS cases have been documented, the annual global burden of HFRS is estimated to exceed 100,000 cases. Because hantavirus infections are frequently underdiagnosed and underreported in many regions, additional unidentified hantaviruses may still exist [3]. Morphological characteristics and genomic structure According to the international committee of taxonomy of viruses, the hantaviruses now belong to the family Hantaviridae of the Class Bunyaviricetes [2]. The virion of hantaviruses is spherical with a diameter of 120–160 nm (Fig. 2).The viral envelop is composed of a 5 nm thickness lipid bilayer containing embedded glycoprotein surface spikes extending to 10 nm from the lipid bilayer [5]. Similar to most of the members of the Class Bunyaviricetes, the hantavirus genome consists of three single-stranded, negative-sense RNA segments designated as large (L), medium (M), and small (S). The L segment encodes the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which is essential for viral RNA transcription and genome replication. The M segment produces a glycoprotein precursor that undergoes cleavage by host-cell signal peptidases to generate the surface glycoproteins Gn and Gc. These glycoproteins mediate attachment to host-cell receptors and play a major role in triggering immune activation and antibody production. The S segment encodes the nucleocapsid (N) protein, which play a role in stabilization of viral RNA. In some hantaviruses, including ANDV, PUUV, SNV, and Tula virus, the S genomic segment encodes a nonstructural protein capable of suppressing interferon production within host cells [7]. Cell entery and viral replication Hantavirus infection begins with the attachment of viral glycoprotein surface spikes to specific receptors on host cell membranes (Fig. 3). In certain hantaviruses, cell entry may also involve integrins, which are transmembrane heterodimeric glycoproteins. After receptor binding, the virus enters host cells through endocytic pathways or macropinocytosis, which may be clathrin-, caveolin-, or cholesterol-dependent. For example, HTNV and SEOV primarily utilize clathrin-mediated endocytosis, whereas ANDV can enter cells via cholesterol-dependent macropinocytosis [8]. After entering the host cell, virions become enclosed within clathrin-coated vesicles originating from specialized clathrin-coated areas of the plasma membrane [9]. The particles are then trafficked sequentially to early endosomes and subsequently to late endosomal and lysosomal compartments. Fusion of the viral envelope with the endosomal membrane enables the release of viral ribonucleoprotein complexes into the cytoplasm, where RdRp mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Messenger RNAs transcribed from the L and S segments are translated by free ribosomes in the cytoplasm, while those derived from the M segment are translated on membrane-bound ribosomes associated with the endoplasmic reticulum and rough endoplasmic reticulum. Newly synthesized virions are assembled within the Golgi apparatus and are thereafter transported to the cell surface for release via exocytosis [10]. Transmission, ecology and epidemiology Although hantaviruses are susceptible to inactivation by heating at 60 °C for 30 min, exposure to ultraviolet light, and treatment with organic or hypochlorite-based solvents [3], they remain relatively stable in environmental conditions. The viruses are capable of surviving for approximately 10 days at room temperature and for about 18 days at lower temperatures such as 4 °C [11], which contributes significantly to their potential transmission. In contrast to many other bunyaviruses, which are spread by insect vectors, hantaviruses infecting humans are primarily rodent-borne [12]. Most hantavirus infections in rodents are asymptomatic and persistent. An exception is infection of Syrian hamsters with ANDV, which can produce clinical manifestations resembling HCPS [13]. Several small mammals, including rodents, moles and shrews act as natural reservoirs for distinct hantavirus species and are capable of transmitting the virus through both horizontal and vertical routes (Fig. 4). Transmission among rodents occurs through droplet or aerosols generated from contaminated secretions and excreta such as urine, feces, and saliva, as well as contaminated food, biting, and scratching [13]. Fecal–oral transmission has also been documented, and infected females may pass the virus transplacentally to their offspring [6]. Behaviors such as mating and fighting further facilitate viral spread among susceptible animals. Humans typically acquire hantavirus infection through inhalation of aerosolized particles contaminated with rodent excreta or through ingestion of contaminated food [14]. Bites or scratches also represent possible routes of human transmission [6]. ANDV is currently the only hantavirus known to spread directly from person to person, usually after close contact with infected individuals .The virus may be present in saliva and transmitted through respiratory droplets generated by coughing or sneezing [15]. Evidence also suggests that ANDV can be transmitted via breast milk, as well as through placental and digestive routes [16]. Additional studies indicate that mites may contribute to the transmission of HFRS to both animals and humans [17]. Pigs have also been shown to acquire HTNV infection without developing severe disease [18]. Antibodies against HTNV and PUUV have additionally been identified in other animals like rabbits and cattle, while SNV antibodies have been detected in dogs and cats [19]. Nevertheless, the ecological significance of these animal hosts in hantavirus transmission remains unclear. The geographic patterns of human hantavirus infections closely correspond to the distribution of their rodent reservoir hosts. Consequently, HFRS is predominantly reported in Europe and Asia, caused by the so-called “old world hantaviruses”, whereas HCPS has been documented exclusively in the Americas, with specific hantavirus species, the so-called “new world hantaviruses” occurring in regions where their natural hosts are prevalent [20]. Currently, the annual global incidence of HFRS is estimated at approximately 100,000 cases. China remains the most highly endemic country, contributing nearly 70–90% of all reported HFRS cases, with about 40,000–60,000 cases recorded annually in recent years [21]. Nevertheless, the actual burden of infection is likely underestimated, as asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic cases probably exceed the number of clinically apparent infections. Furthermore, hantavirus infection is not classified as a notifiable disease in all countries where human cases have been identified [22]. Epidemiology of old world hantaviruses To date, seven hantavirus species have been linked to HFRS .In Asia, the most severe forms of the disease are mainly attributed to HTNV and Amur/Soochong virus infections, with case fatality of approximately 15%. By contrast, SEOV infection is generally associated with less severe clinical course and a lower case fatality rate of about 1–2%. Each year, an estimated 40,000–60,000 HFRS cases are documented, with nearly 99% reported from China [3]. Across Europe, more than 9,000 HFRS cases are reported annually, the majority resulting from PUUV infection. PUUV is widely distributed throughout northern and central Europe, the Balkan Peninsula, and parts of Russia, corresponding to the habitat of its rodent reservoir, Myodes glareolus. Infection with PUUV typically produces the milder form NE. The highest numbers of PUUV-related cases have been recorded in Finland, Sweden, and regions of European Russia. During recent years, reports of NE cases are increasing in Germany and Belgium, with additional cases reported in Norway, France, Austria, Hungary, Slovenia, and neighboring countries [23]. In Europe, severe HFRS cases are mainly caused by DOBV carried by Apodemus flavicollis. Although the geographic distribution of this rodent host extends beyond southeastern Europe, severe DOBV-associated infections have thus far been recognized primarily within the Balkan region [24]. Another hantavirus lineage, SAAV (DOBV-Aa), which is associated with Apodemus agrarius, has been identified as the cause of a comparatively mild form of HFRS in countries including Russia and Slovakia .Unlike DOBV-associated HFRS in the Balkans, where mortality rates may approach 12% ,fatal outcomes have not been reported in SAAV-related infections. More recently, an additional DOBV lineage was detected in Apodemus ponticus rodents from the Sochi region of European Russia and was linked to intermediate severity of HFRS manifestations. The variation in disease severity among the genetically related DOBV lineages may partly reflect differences in their respective rodent reservoir hosts [6]. Tula virus has also been implicated in human disease in Germany, Switzerland, and the Czech Republic; however, definitive evidence confirming its pathogenic role in humans remains limited [6]. Serological investigations have further identified evidence of human hantavirus exposure in countries such as Italy, Latvia, Lithuania, Moldova, Spain, and the United Kingdom .Nevertheless, confirmed clinical cases have not yet been formally documented in these regions [25]. Epidemiology of new world hantaviruses HCPS was first identified as a hantavirus-associated disease in 1993 in the Four Corners region of the United States. Since that initial outbreak, confirmed HCPS cases have also been documented in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Panama, Paraguay, and Uruguay. At least 15 hantavirus species have been associated with HCPS in the Americas. Approximately 200 cases are reported annually across North and South America combined. Although the incidence of HCPS is considerably lower than that of HFRS, the syndrome is associated with a substantially higher mortality rate, averaging nearly 40%. SNV represents the principal etiologic agent of HCPS in the United States, whereas ANDV is the predominant causative virus in South America [20]. Importantly, ANDV remains the only hantavirus conclusively shown to undergo person-to-person transmission, and infections are associated with high fatality rates [26]. Similar to HFRS-associated hantaviruses, HCPS-related viruses may also produce asymptomatic or subclinical infections that remain unrecognized. Supporting this observation, several seroepidemiological studies have demonstrated relatively high antibody prevalence in certain populations despite the low number of clinically diagnosed HCPS cases [25]. Hantavirus infection in Africa The first serological indications of hantavirus infection in Africa were documented in 1984, when Gonzalez et al. detected hantavirus antibodies in rodents and humans from the Central African Republic, Gabon, Burkina Faso, and Benin [27]. Later investigations also provided serological evidence of human exposure in several other African countries, including Guinea, Senegal, Nigeria, Djibouti, and Egypt [25]. Nevertheless, despite these seroepidemiological findings, the literature has reported only a single suspected African case of HFRS to date [28] Pathogenesis of hantaviruses The severity of hantavirus infection in humans differs according to the viral species and strain involved [14]. HFRS predominantly involves the kidneys and vascular system, HCPS mainly targets the lungs and heart. Additional organs, including the liver, spleen, and nervous system, may also be affected. In both humans and animals, hantaviruses primarily infect the endothelial cells in the lung or the kidney as well as macrophages [29]. The principal pathological features of the described hantavirus diseases include increased vascular permeability and acute decrease of the platelet count [3]. Infection is initiated through binding of the viral glycoproteins Gn and Gc to β-integrin receptors, such as αvβ1 and αvβ3, located on the membranes of target cells [29]. Immature dendritic cells situated near endothelial cells transport virions from lymphatic vessels to regional lymph nodes, facilitating further infection of endothelial cells. These dendritic cells also function as antigen-presenting cells, triggering immune responses involving macrophages and CD8 + T lymphocytes [3]. A delayed type I interferon response contributes to elevated viral loads. Following immune activation, cytotoxic T cells release pro-inflammatory cytokines that damage infected endothelial cells, thereby increasing vascular permeability and promoting inflammation [29]. In addition, complement activation and secretion of inflammatory mediators—including interferons, interleukins such as IL-1, IL-6, and IL-10, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) have major roles in altering endothelial barrier function. The nature and strenghth of the immune response vary among different hantavirus species.Patients with HFRS commonly exhibit increased levels of CD8 + T lymphocytes. Elevated concentrations of IL-6 have been linked to thrombocytopenia and impaired renal function [30]. Human leukocyte antigen (HLA) molecules play an essential role in presenting vira…

analysis.background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns about the pandemic potential of hantaviruses because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proven human‑to‑human transmission.
analysis.methods: The authors conducted a narrative review, comparing ANDV outbreaks with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens and focusing on zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability and environmental influences.
analysis.results: ANDV can transmit between humans under close‑contact conditions (A5); the MV Hondius outbreak resulted in 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, giving an approximate case‑fatality rate of 27 % (F10); hantaviruses share pandemic‑associated features such as segmented negative‑sense RNA, rodent reservoirs and environmental drivers (A6).
analysis.contribution: The review underscores that hantaviruses possess several traits linked to pandemic risk, highlighting the need for heightened surveillance and preparedness for possible human‑to‑human spread.
analysis.limitations: The paper is a review without new primary data and relies on reported outbreaks, limiting quantitative risk assessment.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus raised concerns because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proven human-to-human transmission. Methods: A narrative review compared ANDV outbreaks with past pandemics and WHO priority pathogens, emphasizing zoonotic origin, RNA variability and environmental factors. Results: ANDV can spread between humans under close contact (A5); the outbreak produced 11 cases and 3 deaths (~27% CFR) (F10); hantaviruses share pandemic‑associated traits (A6). Contribution: The review highlights pandemic‑related features of hantaviruses, urging stronger surveillance and preparedness. Limitations: It is a review lacking new empirical data and depends on reported cases, restricting quantitative risk evaluation.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 会引起流行病吗?

analysis_zh.background: 2026 年 MV Hondius 爆发的 安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 再次引发了人们对 汉坦病毒es 的大流行潜力的担忧,因为 ANDV 是唯一已证明可人际传播的 汉坦病毒。
analysis_zh.methods: 作者进行了一项叙述性综述,将ANDV疫情与既往大流行及世卫组织重点病原体进行比较,重点关注其动物源起源、RNA基因组变异性和环境影响。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该综述强调,汉坦病毒es具备多项与大流行风险相关的特征,凸显加强监测和为可能的人际传播做好防备的必要性。
analysis_zh.limitations: 本文为综述性文章,未提供新原始数据,且仅基于已报告的疫情,限制了定量风险评估。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：2026 年 MV Hondius 爆发的 安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 再次引发了人们对 汉坦病毒es 的大流行潜力的担忧,因为 ANDV 是唯… 方法：作者进行了一项叙述性综述,将ANDV疫情与既往大流行及世卫组织重点病原体进行比较,重点关注其动物源起源、RNA基因组变异性和环境影响。 结果：未报告 贡献：该综述强调,汉坦病毒es具备多项与大流行风险相关的特征,凸显加强监测和为可能的人际传播做好防备的必要性。 局限：本文为综述性文章,未提供新原始数据,且仅基于已报告的疫情,限制了定量风险评估。

papers.title: Hantavirus Diseases: A Review of Virology, Clinical Features, and Therapeutic Approaches
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic infections that cause two main human diseases: Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS) in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in the Americas . These diseases are acquired mostly by inhaling aerosolized excreta from infected rodent reservoirs but transmission may also occur by direct contact or, rarely, person-to-person transmission with some South American strains. Hantaviruses produce severe morbidity and mortality, the severity of which depends on the viral species, the host immunological response, and the timeliness of medical assistance. Much has been learned over the last several decades from large-scale studies on the distribution of reservoir hosts, environmental determinants of virus persistence, and spillover into human populations that provide essential insight into hantavirus ecology. Significant progress has also been achieved in understanding molecular virology and immunopathogenesis, revealing that much of the clinical severity is due to immune-mediated vascular leakage rather than direct viral cytopathic effects. Yet despite the scientific improvements, there are still big gaps. There are no licensed vaccines for global use and antiviral treatments are limited in scope and efficacy with supportive care being the mainstay of clinical management . Diagnostic methods are improving, but species-specific identification is problematic in locations where numerous hantaviruses cocirculate. Furthermore, environmental change, urbanization, and changing rodent population dynamics are providing more opportunities for viral development and global spread. analysis.background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact.
analysis.main_directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta, immune‑mediated pathogenesis, lack of licensed vaccines, diagnostic challenges, and the influence of environmental change on spillover.
analysis.current_state: Severity varies with viral species, host immune response, and timeliness of care, and current evidence indicates immune‑driven vascular leakage rather than direct cytopathic damage as the main cause of disease.
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited antiviral options, difficulty in species‑specific diagnosis, and uncertainties about how ecological changes affect emergence.
analysis.future_research: Future work should expand large‑scale ecological surveillance of reservoirs, refine diagnostics, and develop vaccines and targeted antivirals to reduce the global burden.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing HFRS in Eurasia and HPS in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact. Main directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta, immune‑mediated pathogenesis, lack of licensed vaccines, diagnostic challenges, and the influence of environmental change on spillover. Current state: Severity varies with viral species, host immune response, and treatment timing, and evidence shows immune‑driven vascular leakage rather than direct cytopathic damage as the main cause. Gaps: Key gaps include absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited antiviral options, difficulty in species‑specific diagnosis, and uncertainties about ecological drivers of emergence. Future research: Priorities are large‑scale reservoir surveillance, improved diagnostics, and development of vaccines and targeted antivirals.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 疾病:病毒学、临床特征和治疗方法综述

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒 是世界范围内的人畜共患病原体,在欧亚大陆引起 肾综合征出血热,在美洲引起 汉坦病毒肺综合征,因此需要对其影响进行审查。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 该综述讨论了通过气溶胶化的啮齿动物排泄物传播、免疫介导的发病机制、缺乏获批疫苗、诊断挑战以及环境变化对溢出事件的影响。
analysis_zh.current_state: 疾病严重程度随病毒种类、宿主免疫反应和治疗时机而异,当前证据表明免疫驱动的血管渗漏而非直接细胞毒性损伤是主要致病机制。
analysis_zh.gaps: 主要缺口包括缺乏全球获批的疫苗、抗病毒药物选择有限、种特异性诊断困难,以及对生态变化如何影响新发的未知数。
analysis_zh.future_research: 未来工作应扩大对宿主储备的大规模生态监测,完善诊断方法,并研发疫苗和靶向抗病毒药物,以降低全球负担。

papers.title: Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak
papers.abstract: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land-based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Although hantavirus infection is classically associated with exposure to infected rodents or contaminated environments, Andes virus is exceptional among orthohantaviruses because limited person-to-person transmission has been documented, particularly after close and prolonged contact. This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition-era travel medicine. Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expedition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability-oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedition cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition-specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark.

Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expedition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability-oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedition cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition-specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark.

Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak Esteban Ortiz-Prado 1*, María Paz Cadena 1, María A. Vethencourt-Ysea 2, Jorge Vasconez-Gonzalez 1, Kirk O. Douglas 3 and Juan S. Izquierdo-Condoy 1 1One Health Research Group, Universidad de Las Americas, Quito, Ecuador, 2Universidad Latina de Costa Rica, San José, Costa Rica, 3Centre for Biosecurity Studies, The University of the West Indies, St. Michael, Barbados The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land-based zoonotic exposure can become a mul­ tinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard envi­ ronments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Although hantavirus infection is classically associated with exposure to infected rodents or contaminated environments, Andes virus is exceptional among orthohantaviruses because limited person- to-person transmission has been documented, particularly after close and pro­ longed contact. This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition-era travel medicine. Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expe­ dition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recogni­ tion of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability- oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syn­ dromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedi­ tion cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition- specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark. KEYWORDS Andes virus, ecological tourism, expedition cruises, One Health, orthohantavirus, travel-associated infections Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 02 frontiersin.org 1 Introduction The recent Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise should be interpreted as more than an iso­ lated maritime health event. It represents a rare and highly instructive One Health case study in which ecological exposure, international tourism, wildlife-associated environments, enclosed transport sys­ tems, delayed diagnosis, intensive care needs, and multi-country public health coordination converged within a single epidemiological chain. The event illustrates how a pathogen usually embedded in regional rodent–environment interfaces can become internationally disruptive when introduced into high-mobility travel networks. On 2 May 2026, the World Health Organization (WHO) was noti­ fied of a cluster of severe acute respiratory illness aboard the Dutch- flagged expedition cruise ship MV Hondius (1). By 25 May 2026, the outbreak linked to the MV Hondius had increased to 13 reported cases and three deaths, corresponding to an observed case fatality ratio of 25% (2). No additional deaths had been reported since 2 May, but more than 600 contacts across 30 countries remained under public health monitoring. Spain reported the most recent additional case in a traveler who was evacuated from the cruise and is isolating at a hos­ pital in Madrid for hantavirus (3). Earlier official reports had docu­ mented six laboratory-confirmed Andes virus infections by PCR or sequencing and two probable cases; subsequent updates expanded the number of reported cases as additional infections were identified among repatriated passengers and crew. The vessel had departed Ushuaia, Argentina, on 1 April 2026, after several passengers had trav­ elled through areas of Argentina, Chile, and Uruguay before boarding (2). The initial WHO report described the probable index case as an adult male who had travelled through the Southern Cone for more than 3 months before embarkation, developed symptoms on 6 April, and died onboard on 11 April. His close contact and travel companion later deteriorated after disembarkation and died in Johannesburg on 26 April; she was subsequently confirmed by PCR as infected with hantavirus. Subsequent clinical and genomic investigations confirmed Andes virus infection in additional cases, with early sequencing closely matching previously reported Argentine sequences, supporting a likely zoonotic introduction before departure while leaving open the contribution of limited person-to-person transmission aboard the vessel (1). This distinction is important because Andes virus is the only orthohantavirus with documented person-to-person transmis­ sion, typically after close and prolonged contact (1, 4). Orthohantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained primarily in rodent reservoirs (5). Human infection usually occurs through inhala­ tion of aerosolized particles from rodent urine, feces, or saliva, par­ ticularly in contaminated enclosed spaces or heavily infested environments (6). Clinically, orthohantavirus infections are classically associated with two major syndromic patterns: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, mainly reported in Europe and Asia, and hanta­ virus cardiopulmonary syndrome, predominantly described in the Americas; nephropathia epidemica is generally considered a milder form of hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (7, 8). In the Americas, orthohantavirus infection can lead to hantavirus cardiopul­ monary syndrome, a severe illness characterized by an initial febrile or gastrointestinal phase followed, in some cases, by rapid cardiopul­ monary deterioration (9, 10). This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in outbreak preparedness for expedition cruises. Rather than proposing a framework that duplicates existing IHR 2005, WHO, or ECDC mechanisms, we ask whether these mechanisms are sufficiently operational for remote, high-mobility, expedition-specific contexts. The outbreak exposed two critical vulnerabilities: the absence of binding, pre-arranged protocols for safe port access and medical evacuation of vessels with suspected or confirmed high-conse­ quence infections, and the lack of a standardized passenger trace­ ability system capable of supporting rapid post-disembarkation monitoring across multiple jurisdictions. The central lesson is therefore not only that expedition cruises require One Health awareness, but that this awareness must be translated into enforce­ able, accountable, and auditable preparedness arrangements before departure. 2 Cruise ships as mobile mass-gathering platforms: enclosed, crowded, isolated and outbreak-prone Cruise ships are not merely transport platforms, they are tempo­ rary mobile communities involving passengers and crew who share cabins, dining areas, corridors, recreational spaces, medical facilities, ventilation systems, sanitation infrastructure, and disembarkation pathways (11, 12). Expedition cruises add further complexity as they deliberately move passengers into remote, wildlife diverse, and often under-surveilled environments where human–animal–environment interfaces are intense and public health infrastructure may be limited (13, 14). From an epidemiological perspective, cruise ships can combine several outbreak-promoting conditions. First, high-density human interaction in semi-enclosed spaces is facilitated. Second, a significant proportion of older passengers, many of whom may have comorbidi­ ties, increasing the risk of severe outcomes after infection are repeat cruise passengers (15). Third, their operations are often far from ter­ tiary hospitals, making diagnosis, isolation, evacuation, and access to intensive care logistically difficult (13). Fourth, they connect multiple jurisdictions within short periods, complicating case investigation, contact tracing, quarantine decisions, and risk communication (15, 16) (Table 1). Finally, due to passenger disembarkment at ports and subsequent repatriation through commercial or charter flights, a shipboard event can rapidly become a multi-country public health challenge. The MV Hondius event illustrates some of these population’s dynamics. The outbreak involved passengers and crew from multiple countries, required medical evacuation, laboratory testing across international networks, and coordination through the International Health Regulations. The long incubation period of Andes virus infection fur­ ther complicated public health management, because exposed indi­ viduals required monitoring for up to 42 days after their last potential exposure (17) (Figure 1). Importantly, the MV Hondius event should not be interpreted as evidence that the cruise ship itself was the ecological source of infec­ tion. Orthohantaviruses are primarily maintained in rodent reser­ voirs, and human infection usually follows environmental exposure to contaminated rodent excreta. In this case, the clinical timeline suggests that the probable index case was likely infected before boarding, most plausibly during travel in the Southern Cone of South Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 03 frontiersin.org America, with symptom onset occurring only after the ship had departed (2). This distinction is essential: the central epidemiological question concerns the pre-boarding exposure history of the index case, particularly during the weeks before embarkation. Nevertheless, once illness developed onboard, the cruise ship became a public health amplifier of complexity. Close quarters, shared spaces, delayed clinical recognition, limited onboard diagnostic capacity, and inter­ national passenger movement transformed a likely land-based zoo­ notic exposure into a multinational surveillance, communication, and response challenge. Thus, the risk posed by cruise ships in such events lies not in serving as the natural reservoir, but in their capacity to complicate detection, containment, contact tracing, and cross- border coordination. This epidemiological context also defines the limits of pre-board­ ing screening. If the probable index case acquired infection before embarkation and was still within the incubation period at the time of boarding, symptom-based screening alone would not have detected the risk. Therefore, the main operational lesson is not that pre-boarding screening should be treated as the primary safeguard, but that expedition cruises require layered detection and escalation systems after departure. These include structured exposure-history assessment, early onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile or gas­ trointestinal illness, immediate isolation triggers, differential diagno­ sis algorithms for severe travel-associated infections, telemedicine consultation, and pre-arranged diagnostic referral pathways. For long-incubation zoonoses, preparedness must extend beyond embar­ kation and continue throughout the voyage and after disembarkation. 3 The global scale of cruise tourism The global cruise industry has reached an unprecedented scale, creating important One Health challenges for zoonotic dis­ ease emergence, detection, and dissemination. By the end of 2025, the worldwide ocean cruise fleet was estimated at approximately 370 ships, with a total passenger capacity of about 704,200 berths (18). Major operators dominate this capacity: Carnival Corporation accounted for approximately 95 ships and 257,120 berths; Royal Caribbean Group for 58 ships and 155,430 berths; and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings, including Oceania Cruises and Regent Seven Seas Cruises, for 34 ships and 71,510 berths. The remaining 183 vessels, including luxury, niche, and expedi­ tion operators, represented approximately 220,120 berths (Figure 2). This scale is epidemiologically relevant. The Cruise Lines International Association reported that global ocean cruise passen­ ger volume reached a historic high of 37. million passengers in 2025 (19), while independent industry estimates projected more than 38 million passengers in 2026 (20). Although mass-market vessels account for most global capacity, the expedition cruise segment including small, specialized ships such as the MV Hondius, with a capacity of approximately 170–196 passengers is expanding in response to demand for immersive nature-based travel. These vessels routinely perform island-hopping itineraries through remote and ecologically sensitive regions, including Patagonia, Antarctica, South Georgia, the South Atlantic islands, and other biodiversity-rich des­ tinations. With continued fleet growth and diversification of cruise itineraries, the frequency of human–animal–environment interfaces in zoonotic hotspots is likely to increase, amplifying the public health implications illustrated by the MV Hondius Andes virus outbreak. 4 Ecological tourism, island hopping and overlooked exposure interfaces Ecological tourism is often framed as low-impact because it is educational, conservation-oriented, or scientifically motivated (21). However, the MV Hondius outbreak underscores that some forms of ecotourism can place travelers in contact with underrecognized zoo­ notic interfaces. Birdwatchers, wildlife photographers, naturalists, expedition guides, and adventure tourists may visit wetlands, forest edges, rural roads, islands, docks, farms, abandoned structures, TABLE 1 The factors, epidemiological relevance and One Health implication of how expedition cruises can amplify infectious disease risk. Cruise- related factor Epidemiological relevance One Health implication High passenger density Frequent close contact in shared indoor spaces Increased risk of transmission for pathogens capable of human-to-human spread Enclosed environments Cabins, dining rooms, lecture halls, corridors, and medical rooms may concentrate exposure Need for ventilation, isolation protocols, and infection prevention measures Older passenger demographics Many expedition cruises attract older adults, sometimes with comorbidities Higher risk of severe disease, hospitalization, and intensive care need Remote navigation Ships may be days away from tertiary hospitals Requires pre-planned evacuation and telemedicine pathways Multi-country itineraries Passengers may disembark in several jurisdictions Requires international contact tracing and IHR coordination Delayed symptom recognition Early hantavirus symptoms may resemble influenza-like or gastrointestinal illness Need for shipboard syndromic surveillance Long incubation period Andes virus symptoms may appear weeks after exposure Requires post-travel monitoring and risk-stratified follow- up Limited onboard diagnostics Ship medical units usually lack advanced pathogen testing Need for rapid molecular platforms and reference- laboratory networks Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 04 frontiersin.org storage areas, rustic cabins, food markets, landfills, and waste-dis­ posal sites (22–24). These settings are transition zones where humans, wildlife, synanthropic animals, rodents, food waste, con­ taminated dust, and environmental pathogens may converge (Figure 3). In the current Andes hantavirus cruise outbreak, the probable index case was described in official reports as an older adult trav­ eler who had spent several months in the Southern Cone before embarkation and developed symptoms after departure (1). The individual is not named here because personal identification does not add to the epidemiological assessment of the outbreak and is not necessary for public health interpretation. Although media reports have discussed possible exposure sites, no definitive source of infection has been confirmed. Therefore, this outbreak should be interpreted cautiously. Its value lies in illustrating how expedition cruises and ecological tourism may create opportuni­ ties for zoonotic exposure and subsequent outbreak amplification in enclosed travel settings, but the available evidence does not confirm a specific source, site of exposure, or transmission path­ way. The landfill hypothesis should not be presented as a con­ firmed source of infection; rather, it is best und…

analysis.background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal.
analysis.methods: This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition‑era travel medicine, drawing on WHO reports and existing guidance.
analysis.results: By 25 May 2026, the outbreak had increased to 13 reported cases and three deaths, yielding an observed case fatality ratio of 25 % and more than 600 contacts across 30 countries; genomic investigations confirmed Andes virus infection and suggested limited person‑to‑person transmission aboard the vessel.
analysis.contribution: The authors propose an accountability‑oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route‑level risk assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference‑laboratory pathways, port‑of‑call agreements, auditable passenger records, and cross‑border post‑travel monitoring.
analysis.limitations: The paper is a perspective and does not present new primary data; it relies on secondary reports and case‑study analysis, limiting generalizability and detailed epidemiologic inference.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Methods: This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition‑era travel medicine, drawing on WHO reports and existing guidance. Results: By 25 May 2026, the outbreak had increased to 13 reported cases and three deaths, yielding an observed case fatality ratio of 25 % and more than 600 contacts across 30 countries; genomic investigations confirmed Andes virus infection and suggested limited person‑to‑person transmission aboard the vessel. Contribution: The authors propose an accountability‑oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route‑level risk assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference‑laboratory pathways, port‑of‑call agreements, auditable passenger records, and cross‑border post‑travel monitoring. Limitations: The paper is a perspective and does not present new primary data; it relies on secondary reports and case‑study analysis, limiting generalizability and detailed epidemiologic inference.

papers.title_zh: 探险游轮、跳岛游和人畜共患病风险:MV Hondius Andes 汉坦病毒 疫情的治理和运营经验教训

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒 正汉坦病毒 疫情与 MV Hondius 探险航线相连,说明一次可能的陆源人畜共患暴露在与封闭船舶环境、临床识别延迟、远程航行、医疗撤离以及国际乘客分散相交叉时,可演变为跨国公共卫生事件。
analysis_zh.methods: 本观点文章以 MV Hondius 疫情为分析案例研究,识别探险时代旅行医学的治理和运营缺口,依据 WHO 报告和现有指南进行分析。
analysis_zh.results: 截至 2026 年 5 月 25 日,疫情已增至 13 例报告病例和 3 例死亡,观察到的病例致死率为 25 %,并在 30 个国家产生 600 多名接触者;基因组研究证实了 安第斯病毒 感染,并提示船上人际传播有限。
analysis_zh.contribution: 作者提出以问责为导向的 One Health 防备模型,通过航线层面的风险评估、船上综合征监测、隔离和远程医疗触发条件、参考实验室路径、停靠港协议、可审计的乘客记录以及跨境旅行后监测,实现现有指南的落地。
analysis_zh.limitations: 本文为观点文章,未提供新的原始数据;依赖二手报告和案例分析,因而限制了其普遍适用性和详细流行病学推断。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:安第斯病毒 正汉坦病毒 疫情与 MV Hondius 探险航线相连,说明一次可能的陆源人畜共患... 方法:本观点文章以 MV Hondius 疫情为分析案例研究,识别探险时代旅行医学的治理和运营缺口,... 结果:截至 2026 年 5 月 25 日,疫情已增至 13 例报告病例和 3 例死亡,观察到的病例... 贡献:作者提出以问责为导向的 One Health 防备模型,通过航线层面的风险评估、船上综合征监测... 局限:本文为观点文章,未提供新的原始数据;依赖二手报告和案例分析,因而限制了其普遍适用性和详细流行病...

papers.title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.
papers.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) is unique among hantaviruses because person-to-person transmission is possible. This raises questions on the relevance of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), particularly following high-risk household, healthcare-associated, or laboratory exposures, considering its high case fatality rate. Ribavirin has demonstrated antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, although clinical evidence supporting its use for ANDV remains extremely limited. This viewpoint summarises the currently available evidence regarding ribavirin as PEP after potential ANDV exposure. We discuss the knowledge gaps that may limit the applicability of ribavirin PEP, to make informed decisions on the use of ribavirin in the setting of PEP. Potential dosing strategies are visualised by modelling and simulation, and potential dose and duration are discussed. Although the biological rationale for ribavirin PEP appears compelling, absence of controlled human studies and potential toxicity currently limit its role to highly selected exposure scenarios and use shortly after exposure.

analysis.background: ANDV is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans, prompting concern about severe outcomes.
analysis.main_directions: The viewpoint examines the relevance of post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP) after high‑risk household, healthcare, or laboratory exposures and explores ribavirin dosing strategies via modelling.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal model activity against hantaviruses, but clinical data for ANDV PEP are extremely limited.
analysis.gaps: Key knowledge gaps include the lack of controlled human studies, uncertain optimal dose and duration, and potential toxicity concerns.
analysis.future_research: Further work should focus on clinical trials to define safe and effective ribavirin PEP regimens and on pharmacokinetic modelling to refine dosing.

analysis.summary_en: Background: ANDV uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising severe disease concerns. Main directions: The article reviews PEP relevance after high‑risk exposures and models ribavirin dosing. Current state: Ribavirin is active in vitro/animal studies but lacks robust clinical evidence for ANDV PEP. Gaps: No controlled human trials, unclear dosing, and toxicity issues remain. Future research: Conduct clinical trials and pharmacokinetic modelling to establish safe, effective ribavirin PEP protocols.

papers.title_zh: 利巴韦林暴露后预防[ PDI0008 ]: 观点

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒是唯一已知在人与人之间传播的 汉坦病毒,引发对严重后果的担忧。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本观点审视高风险家庭、医疗或实验室暴露后进行暴露后预防(PEP)的相关性,并通过建模探讨利巴韦林的剂量方案。
analysis_zh.current_state: 利巴韦林在体外和动物模型中对 汉坦病毒 显示活性,但针对安第斯病毒暴露后预防的临床数据极为有限。
analysis_zh.gaps: 关键知识空白包括缺乏受控的人体研究、最佳剂量和疗程不确定,以及潜在的毒性担忧。
analysis_zh.future_research: 进一步工作应聚焦于临床试验,以确定安全有效的利巴韦林暴露后预防方案,并通过药代动力学建模优化剂量。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：安第斯病毒是唯一已知在人与人之间传播的 汉坦病毒,引发对严重后果的担忧。 主要方向：本观点审视高风险家庭、医疗或实验室暴露后进行暴露后预防(PEP)的相关性,并通过建模探讨利巴韦林的剂量方案。 研究现状：利巴韦林在体外和动物模型中对 汉坦病毒 显示活性,但针对安第斯病毒暴露后预防的临床数据极为有限。 不足：关键知识空白包括缺乏受控的人体研究、最佳剂量和疗程不确定,以及潜在的毒性担忧。 后续研究：进一步工作应聚焦于临床试验,以确定安全有效的利巴韦林暴露后预防方案,并通过药代动力学建模优化剂量。

papers.title: Modeling the Transmission Dynamics of Hantavirus with Environmental Viral Persistence
papers.abstract: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, and human exposure occurs mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles. This study formulates and analyses a deterministic compartmental model for hantavirus transmission involving susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir. The model includes logistic rodent growth, direct rodent-to-rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding from infected classes and natural viral clearance. Fundamental qualitative properties of the system are established, including existence, uniqueness, positivity and boundedness of solutions within a feasible region. The disease-free equilibrium is obtained, and the basic reproduction number is derived using the next-generation matrix method. Stability analysis shows that the disease-free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1, whereas disease persistence is associated with R0 > 1 and the existence of a biologically feasible endemic equilibrium. Local stability conditions for the endemic equilibrium are characterised through the Jacobian matrix and the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. The analysis indicates that environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway that can sustain transmission between infected rodents, susceptible rodents and humans. These findings suggest that reducing rodent infection, limiting environmental contamination and increasing viral clearance may help move the system below the epidemic threshold. The model offers a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and for guiding future extensions involving seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data-driven parameterisation.

analysis.background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles.
analysis.methods: The authors formulate and analyse a deterministic compartmental model that includes susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir.
analysis.results: The model incorporates logistic rodent growth, direct rodent‑to‑rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding and natural viral clearance; the disease‑free equilibrium and basic reproduction number (R0) are derived; stability analysis shows the disease‑free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1 and persistence occurs when R0 > 1 with a feasible endemic equilibrium; environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway sustaining transmission.
analysis.contribution: The work provides a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and suggests targets (rodent infection reduction, environmental decontamination, increased viral clearance) for moving the system below the epidemic threshold, guiding future extensions with seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data‑driven parameterisation.
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; no empirical data, parameter estimation, or model validation are reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure mainly via contaminated environments. Methods: A deterministic compartmental model linking rodents, humans, and an environmental viral reservoir was formulated and analysed. Results: The model yields a basic reproduction number, shows disease‑free equilibrium stability when R0 < 1 and endemic persistence when R0 > 1, and highlights environmental viral persistence as an indirect transmission route. Contribution: This provides a tractable framework for understanding hantavirus persistence and informs control strategies, while offering a basis for future extensions. Limitations: Only an abstract is available; no empirical data, parameterisation, or validation are reported.

papers.title_zh: 利用环境病毒持久性对 汉坦病毒 的传播动力学进行建模

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒是一种主要在啮齿动物宿主中维持的人畜共患感染,人类暴露主要通过被传染性病毒颗粒污染的环境。
analysis_zh.methods: 作者构建并分析了一个确定性分区模型,包含易感和感染的啮齿动物,易感、潜伏、感染和康复的人群,以及环境病毒库。
analysis_zh.results: 该模型包括啮齿动物的逻辑斯蒂增长、直接的啮齿动物间传播、间接的环境传播、病毒脱落和自然病毒清除;推导出无病平衡和基本再生数 (R0);稳定性分析表明,当 R0 < 1 时,无病平衡在局部和全局上渐近稳定;当 R0 > 1 时出现持续性,并存在可行的地方性流行平衡;环境病毒的持续存在提供了维持传播的间接途径。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该研究提供了一个数学上可处理的框架,用于解释 汉坦病毒 的持续性,并提出了降低啮齿动物感染、环境去污、提升病毒清除等干预目标,以将系统推至流行阈值以下,为后续加入季节性、空间结构、随机性和数据驱动参数化的扩展提供指导。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供了摘要;未报告实证数据、参数估计或模型验证。

papers.title: Enfermedades transmitidas por vectores: chagas, dengue, malaria y hantavirus
papers.abstract: Rol estratégico de la Atención Primaria: el personal médico de familia es fundamental para el cribado inicial, el manejo ambulatorio de los casos leves y la prevención del colapso hospitalario. Alta sospecha clínica: el diagnóstico inicial se fundamenta en vincular un síndrome febril inespecífico con un nexo epidemiológico claro (viajes recientes, picaduras o exposición a roedores/brotes cerrados). Identificación de «banderas rojas»: es prioritario reconocer los signos de alarma (como sangrado de mucosas, dolor abdominal intenso, compromiso respiratorio o descompensación cardíaca) para garantizar una derivación hospitalaria inmediata y oportuna. Acción comunitaria: la educación para el control del vector y la notificación epidemiológica son pilares indelegables en el abordaje integral desde el centro de salud.

analysis.background: The paper discusses vector‑borne diseases (Chagas, dengue, malaria, hantavirus) and stresses the strategic role of primary‑care physicians in initial screening, outpatient care of mild cases, and preventing hospital collapse (A1).
analysis.methods: High clinical suspicion is advocated, linking nonspecific febrile syndromes to clear epidemiological links such as recent travel, bites, or rodent exposure (A2).
analysis.results: Priority is given to recognizing red‑flag signs—mucosal bleeding, intense abdominal pain, respiratory compromise, or cardiac decompensation—to trigger immediate hospital referral (A3).
analysis.contribution: The article emphasizes community education for vector control and mandatory epidemiological notification as essential pillars of an integrated primary‑care approach (A4).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is available; detailed methodology, sample size, location, host information, and quantitative results are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The paper discusses vector‑borne diseases and stresses primary‑care physicians' strategic role in early screening and outpatient management (A1). Methods: High clinical suspicion links nonspecific fever to clear epidemiological exposure (A2). Results: Red‑flag signs such as mucosal bleeding, severe abdominal pain, respiratory or cardiac compromise warrant immediate hospital referral (A3). Contribution: Community education for vector control and mandatory epidemiological reporting are highlighted as key components (A4). Limitations: Only an abstract is provided; detailed methods, sample size, location, host data, and quantitative outcomes are not reported.

papers.title_zh: 媒介传播疾病:奇瓦斯病、登革热、疟疾和汉坦病毒

analysis_zh.background: 本文讨论了媒介传播疾病(恰加斯病、登革热、疟疾,汉坦病毒),并强调初级保健医师在初筛、轻症门诊治疗以及防止医院崩溃中的战略作用(A1)。
analysis_zh.methods: 倡导高度临床警觉,将非特异性发热综合征与近期旅行、叮咬或啮齿动物暴露等明确的流行病学联系相结合(A2)。
analysis_zh.results: 优先识别红旗征象——黏膜出血、剧烈腹痛、呼吸受限或心功能不全,以启动即时医院转诊(A3)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 文章强调社区教育以进行媒介控制,并将强制流行病学报告作为综合初级保健方案的关键支柱(A4)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供摘要;未报告详细方法、样本量、地点、宿主信息及定量结果。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：本文讨论了媒介传播疾病(恰加斯病、登革热、疟疾,汉坦病毒),并强调初级保健医师在初筛、轻症门诊治疗以及防止医院崩溃中的战略作用(A1)。 方法：倡导高度临床警觉,将非特异性发热综合征与近期旅行、叮咬或啮齿动物暴露等明确的流行病学联系相结合(A2)。 结果：优先识别红旗征象——黏膜出血、剧烈腹痛、呼吸受限或心功能不全,以启动即时医院转诊(A3)。 贡献：文章强调社区教育以进行媒介控制,并将强制流行病学报告作为综合初级保健方案的关键支柱(A4)。 局限：仅提供摘要;未报告详细方法、样本量、地点、宿主信息及定量结果。

papers.title: First nationwide full-genome characterisation of human-derived Andes virus in Chile: a retrospective genomic epidemiology study.
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina. In Chile, ANDV genomic diversity remains incompletely characterised. This study aimed to characterise the genetic diversity, geographical structure, and molecular signatures of ANDV using human clinical samples collected over a 13-year period (2011-24). METHODS: We conducted a retrospective genomic epidemiology study of ANDV infections in Chile. Clinical samples from patients with confirmed ANDV, collected between March 9, 2011, and June 27, 2024, were analysed and sequenced. Clinical and epidemiological data were obtained from diagnostic laboratories and surveillance programmes. Consensus sequences for the S, M, and L segments were generated, and genetic clustering and divergence were assessed using phylogenetic inference and variant calling. FINDINGS: We analysed clinical samples from 58 infected individuals and identified two major genomic variants of ANDV with distinct geographical distributions, defined by regionally structured patterns of nucleotide and amino acid substitutions across the S, M, and L segments: ANDV Chi-North (central Chile) and ANDV-South (southern Chile). No consistent clustering by clinical severity was observed, and no recurrent non-synonymous substitutions were uniquely associated with severe disease. Substitutions previously associated with person-to-person transmission in outbreaks in Argentina were not consistently observed in Chilean sequences, including in four person-to-person transmission cases. Although some substitutions described in ANDV-like viruses were present in the Chi-North lineage, this lineage remained phylogenetically distinct and geographically restricted to central Chile. INTERPRETATION: To our knowledge, this study provides the first nationwide genomic characterisation of human-derived ANDV in Chile. The identification of geographically structured variants indicates that ANDV diversity in Chile is driven by regional diversification rather than clinical outcome. The absence of consistent amino acid signatures associated with disease severity or person-to-person transmission suggests that these phenotypes are unlikely to be explained by viral genetic variation alone. These findings refine current understanding of ANDV evolution and highlight the need for continued integrated genomic surveillance in endemic regions. FUNDING: Agencia Nacional de Investigación y Desarrollo de Chile and National Institutes of Health.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina; its genomic diversity in Chile remains incompletely characterised (A1, A2, A3).
analysis.methods: A retrospective genomic epidemiology study analysed clinical samples from 58 confirmed ANDV patients collected between 9 Mar 2011 and 27 Jun 2024 (A4, A5); epidemiological data were obtained from diagnostic labs and surveillance programmes (A6); consensus sequences for S, M, and L segments were generated and phylogenetic inference with variant calling assessed genetic clustering and divergence (A7).
analysis.results: Two major genomic variants with distinct geographic distributions were identified: ANDV Chi‑North (central Chile) and ANDV‑South (southern Chile) (A8); no consistent clustering by clinical severity or unique non‑synonymous substitutions linked to severe disease was observed (A9); substitutions previously associated with person‑to‑person transmission in Argentina were not consistently present, including in four Chilean transmission cases (A10); the Chi‑North lineage, while sharing some substitutions with ANDV‑like viruses, remained phylogenetically distinct (A11).
analysis.contribution: The study provides the first nationwide human‑derived ANDV genomic characterisation in Chile (A12); it shows that regional diversification, not clinical outcome, drives viral diversity (A13); the lack of consistent amino‑acid signatures for severity or transmission suggests these phenotypes are unlikely explained solely by viral genetics (A14); findings highlight the need for continued integrated genomic surveillance in endemic regions (A15).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is available, so detailed methods, sampling strategy, sequencing depth, and statistical analyses are not reported; causality cannot be inferred from the associations described.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus uniquely transmits between humans and causes HPS in Chile/Argentina, but its Chilean genomic diversity is incompletely known. Methods: Retrospective study of 58 confirmed cases (2011‑2024) sequenced S, M, L segments with phylogenetic and variant analyses. Results: Two geographically distinct variants (Chi‑North, South) were found; no consistent links to disease severity or transmission‑associated mutations. Contribution: First nationwide human‑derived ANDV genomics in Chile, showing regional diversification drives diversity and that genetics alone likely does not explain severity or person‑to‑person spread, underscoring need for surveillance. Limitations: Abstract‑only data limit detail on methods and prevent causal inference.

papers.title_zh: 人源性安第斯病毒在智利的首次全国全基因组表征:一项回顾性基因组流行病学研究

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 是已知唯一能够在人与人之间传播的 汉坦病毒,并在智利和阿根廷导致 汉坦病毒心肺综合征;其在智利的基因组多样性仍未完全表征 (A1, A2, A3)。
analysis_zh.methods: 一项回顾性基因组流行病学研究分析了在 2011年3月9日至2024年6月27日收集的 58 例确诊 ANDV 患者的临床样本 (A4, A5);流行病学数据来源于诊断实验室和监测项目 (A6);对 S、M、L 片段生成共识序列,并通过变异检测的系统发育推断评估基因聚类和分歧情况 (A7)。
analysis_zh.results: 鉴定出两种具有不同地域分布的主要基因组变体:ANDV Chi‐North(智利中部)和 ANDV‐South(智利南部) (A8);未观察到与临床严重程度相关的稳定聚类或与重症关联的独特非同义置换 (A9);先前在阿根廷与人际传播相关的置换在本研究中未一致出现,包括四例智利传播病例 (A10);Chi‐North 系统虽与部分 ANDV 类病毒共享某些置换,但在系统发育上仍保持独立 (A11)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 本研究提供了智利首个全国范围的人源 ANDV 基因组特征描述 (A12);结果表明区域分化而非临床结局驱动病毒多样性 (A13);缺乏与严重程度或传播相关的统一氨基酸特征提示这些表型不太可能仅由病毒遗传决定 (A14);研究强调在流行地区持续开展综合基因组监测的必要性 (A15)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供摘要,未报告详细方法、取样策略、测序深度和统计分析;无法根据所述关联推断因果关系。

papers.title: Bleeding in haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome: A systematic review characterising the loss of haemostasis in hantavirus infections.
papers.abstract: Background Haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is caused by hantaviruses and is associated with variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding manifestations. While clinical features have been described in multiple studies, no previous systematic review has synthesised evidence on haemostatic dysfunction in HFRS. This review aimed to characterise the clinical and laboratory features of coagulation disturbances in HFRS to inform understanding of disease mechanisms and future research. Methodology/principal findings We systematically searched MEDLINE, PubMed, CINAHL, Web of Science, and Scopus on 12 December 2024 for studies reporting clinical or laboratory evidence of haemostatic dysfunction in confirmed HFRS. The review protocol was prospectively registered on PROSPERO (CRD42024618760). Observational studies and case series meeting predefined inclusion criteria were eligible. Risk of bias was assessed using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal tools and, for one before-after cohort study, the National Institutes of Health (NIH) quality assessment tool. Studies were graded from 1 (lowest) to 3 (highest) based on methodological quality. Due to heterogeneity in study design and outcome reporting, meta-analysis was not feasible. Weighted averages were calculated for laboratory parameters, and data were visualised using bubble and bar plots. Fifty-five studies comprising 7,950 patients were included. Thrombocytopenia was the most consistent abnormality, typically more pronounced in infections with Hantaan (HTNV), Dobrava (DOBV), and Seoul (SEOV) viruses compared with Puumala (PUUV), which was associated with milder disease. Mild prolongation of activated partial thromboplastin time and elevated D-dimer were variably reported. Limited paediatric data suggested milder disease and less pronounced coagulation abnormalities. Conclusions/significance This review provides the first systematic synthesis of haemostatic dysfunction in HFRS. Findings highlight more severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV infections compared with PUUV. Variable study quality and incomplete reporting limit firm conclusions, underscoring the need for standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes.

Thrombocytopenia was the most consistent abnormality, typically more pronounced in infections with Hantaan (HTNV), Dobrava (DOBV), and Seoul (SEOV) viruses compared with Puumala (PUUV), which was associated with milder disease. Mild prolongation of activated partial thromboplastin time and elevated D-dimer were variably reported. Limited paediatric data suggested milder disease and less pronounced coagulation abnormalities. Conclusions/significance This review provides the first systematic synthesis of haemostatic dysfunction in HFRS. Findings highlight more severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV infections compared with PUUV. Variable study quality and incomplete reporting limit firm conclusions, underscoring the need for standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes.

source_records.title: Bleeding in haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome: A systematic review characterising the loss of haemostasis in hantavirus infections.
source_records.abstract: Background Haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is caused by hantaviruses and is associated with variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding manifestations. While clinical features have been described in multiple studies, no previous systematic review has synthesised evidence on haemostatic dysfunction in HFRS. This review aimed to characterise the clinical and laboratory features of coagulation disturbances in HFRS to inform understanding of disease mechanisms and future research. Methodology/principal findings We systematically searched MEDLINE, PubMed, CINAHL, Web of Science, and Scopus on 12 December 2024 for studies reporting clinical or laboratory evidence of haemostatic dysfunction in confirmed HFRS. The review protocol was prospectively registered on PROSPERO (CRD42024618760). Observational studies and case series meeting predefined inclusion criteria were eligible. Risk of bias was assessed using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal tools and, for one before-after cohort study, the National Institutes of Health (NIH) quality assessment tool. Studies were graded from 1 (lowest) to 3 (highest) based on methodological quality. Due to heterogeneity in study design and outcome reporting, meta-analysis was not feasible. Weighted averages were calculated for laboratory parameters, and data were visualised using bubble and bar plots. Fifty-five studies comprising 7,950 patients were included. Thrombocytopenia was the most consistent abnormality, typically more pronounced in infections with Hantaan (HTNV), Dobrava (DOBV), and Seoul (SEOV) viruses compared with Puumala (PUUV), which was associated with milder disease. Mild prolongation of activated partial thromboplastin time and elevated D-dimer were variably reported. Limited paediatric data suggested milder disease and less pronounced coagulation abnormalities. Conclusions/significance This review provides the first systematic synthesis of haemostatic dysfunction in HFRS. Findings highlight more severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV infections compared with PUUV. Variable study quality and incomplete reporting limit firm conclusions, underscoring the need for standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes.

analysis.background: HFRS caused by hantaviruses presents with thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding, yet no systematic synthesis of haemostatic dysfunction existed.
analysis.main_directions: The review collates clinical and laboratory data on coagulation disturbances across hantavirus species, highlighting differences in thrombocytopenia severity (HTNV, DOBV, SEOV vs PUUV), modest aPTT prolongation, D‑dimer elevation, and limited paediatric observations.
analysis.current_state: From 55 studies (≈7,950 patients), thrombocytopenia is the most consistent finding, especially with HTNV, DOBV, SEOV; coagulation tests show mild aPTT prolongation and variable D‑dimer rise, while children appear to experience milder disease.
analysis.gaps: Heterogeneity in study designs and outcome reporting precluded meta‑analysis, and variable methodological quality and incomplete data limit definitive conclusions.
analysis.future_research: Prospective, standardised investigations of coagulation parameters and bleeding outcomes across hantavirus genotypes, including pediatric cohorts, are required.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HFRS caused by hantaviruses presents with thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding, yet no systematic synthesis existed. Main directions: The review aggregates clinical and lab data on coagulation disturbances, noting species‑specific thrombocytopenia severity, mild aPTT prolongation, D‑dimer changes, and scarce paediatric data. Current state: Across 55 studies (~7,950 patients), thrombocytopenia is the hallmark, especially with HTNV, DOBV, SEOV; coagulation tests show modest changes, and children tend to have milder disease. Gaps: Study heterogeneity and variable quality prevented meta‑analysis and limit firm conclusions. Future research: Standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes across hantavirus genotypes, including children, are needed.

papers.title_zh: 肾综合征出血热出血:关于汉坦病毒感染中止血功能丧失的系统性综述。

analysis_zh.background: 由 汉坦病毒 引起的肾综合征出血热表现为血小板减少、凝血异常和出血,但尚无系统性血液止血功能障碍的综合分析。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述汇总了跨 汉坦病毒 物种的凝血紊乱的临床和实验室数据,突出血小板减少严重程度的差异(汉滩病毒、DOBV、首尔病毒 对比 普马拉病毒)、轻度aPTT延长、D‐dimer升高,以及儿科观察数据有限。
analysis_zh.current_state: 来自55项研究(约7,950例患者),血小板减少是最一致的发现,尤其在汉滩病毒、DOBV、首尔病毒感染者中;凝血检测显示轻度aPTT延长和D‐dimer水平的可变升高,而儿童似乎病情较轻。
analysis_zh.gaps: 研究设计和结局报告的异质性阻碍了荟萃分析,且方法学质量参差不齐、数据不完整限制了明确结论的得出。
analysis_zh.future_research: 需要前瞻性、标准化的研究,针对跨 汉坦病毒 基因型的凝血指标和出血结局进行调查,并纳入儿科队列。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:由 汉坦病毒 引起的肾综合征出血热表现为血小板减少、凝血异常和出血,但尚无系统性血液止血功能障... 主要方向:本综述汇总了跨 汉坦病毒 物种的凝血紊乱的临床和实验室数据,突出血小板减少严重程度的差异(汉滩... 研究现状:来自55项研究(约7,950例患者),血小板减少是最一致的发现,尤其在汉滩病毒、DOBV、首尔... 不足:研究设计和结局报告的异质性阻碍了荟萃分析,且方法学质量参差不齐、数据不完整限制了明确结论的得出。 后续研究:需要前瞻性、标准化的研究,针对跨 汉坦病毒 基因型的凝血指标和出血结局进行调查,并纳入儿科队列。

papers.title: Andes Virus - A Clinical Review.
papers.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) is the sole orthohantavirus with documented human-to-human transmission. We summarize the epidemiology and clinical features of ANDV infection and review best practices in clinical management, as based on published expert consensus guidelines, field experience, and clinical trials. We also evaluate currently available and investigational treatments (including the use of antiviral agents), assess emerging monoclonal antibody therapies, and outline prospects for vaccine development. Finally, we discuss important infection prevention and control measures.

source_records.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) is the sole orthohantavirus with documented human-to-human transmission. analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the sole orthohantavirus with documented human‑to‑human transmission.
analysis.main_directions: The review summarizes ANDV epidemiology, clinical presentation, and best‑practice management, and evaluates antiviral, monoclonal antibody, and vaccine approaches.
analysis.current_state: Clinical care relies on expert consensus and limited trial data; investigational monoclonal antibodies and early vaccine concepts are described.
analysis.gaps: Evidence for effective antiviral regimens, long‑term outcomes, and rigorously evaluated infection‑control measures is weak.
analysis.future_research: Randomized trials of antivirals and monoclonal antibodies, development and testing of vaccines, and systematic assessment of prevention strategies are required.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus is the only orthohantavirus known to spread between humans. Main directions: The article reviews epidemiology, clinical features, management guidelines, and therapeutic/vaccine prospects. Current state: Care is based on expert consensus with limited trial data; monoclonal antibodies and vaccine candidates are emerging. Gaps: Robust evidence for antivirals, outcomes, and infection‑control practices is lacking. Future research: Conduct controlled trials of antivirals and antibodies, advance vaccine development, and evaluate prevention measures.

papers.title_zh: 安第斯病毒 - 临床综述。

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒(安第斯病毒)是唯一已记录有人际传播的正汉坦病毒。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述概述安第斯病毒的流行病学、临床表现和最佳实践管理,并评估抗病毒药物、单克隆抗体和疫苗方案。
analysis_zh.current_state: 临床护理依赖专家共识和有限的试验数据;文中描述了正在研究的单克隆抗体和早期疫苗概念。
analysis_zh.gaps: 对有效抗病毒方案、长期结局以及严格评估的感染控制措施的证据薄弱。
analysis_zh.future_research: 需要开展抗病毒药物和单克隆抗体的随机试验、疫苗的研发与测试,以及对预防策略的系统评估。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：安第斯病毒(安第斯病毒)是唯一已记录有人际传播的正汉坦病毒。 主要方向：本综述概述安第斯病毒的流行病学、临床表现和最佳实践管理,并评估抗病毒药物、单克隆抗体和疫苗方案。 研究现状：临床护理依赖专家共识和有限的试验数据;文中描述了正在研究的单克隆抗体和早期疫苗概念。 不足：对有效抗病毒方案、长期结局以及严格评估的感染控制措施的证据薄弱。 后续研究：需要开展抗病毒药物和单克隆抗体的随机试验、疫苗的研发与测试,以及对预防策略的系统评估。
